title: Customer Responsibilities - Philadelphia Water Department
claim_notes: PWD states customers must repair faulty plumbing connecting water and sewer mains to their home, including the sewer lateral.
trust_notes: Official utility responsibility source.

title: Water customer responsibilities | City of Philadelphia
claim_notes: City of Philadelphia says customers maintain and repair sanitary and storm sewer lateral lines connecting the house to the sewer main.
trust_notes: Official city services page with current phrasing.

title: Report a Sewer Problem | Portland.gov
claim_notes: Portland says if the problem is in the line from home to street, repair is the property owner's responsibility; if in the City sewer line, it is the City's responsibility.
trust_notes: Useful to show city-by-city boundary differences.

title: Broken Sewer and Drain Lines - Guide to Repairs, Permits and Inspections | Portland.gov
claim_notes: Portland BDS says the owner is responsible for breaks between the house and curb, while the City maintains laterals from the main to the curb line.
trust_notes: Adds permitting and right-of-way nuance.

title: Service Line Protection Program - DEP
claim_notes: NYC DEP says owners are responsible for water and sanitary sewer service lines to the mains, and describes optional protection with exclusions and limitations.
trust_notes: Strong official source tying owner responsibility to optional protection.

title: What Is Service Line Coverage? | Progressive
claim_notes: Progressive describes service line coverage as an endorsement added to many policies, often with a deductible and limits that vary by insurer and state.
trust_notes: Useful for caveat language around endorsements and exclusions.

title: A broken pipe can break the bank, who is responsible for service line damage? | Nationwide
claim_notes: Nationwide states standard homeowners policies do not cover service lines unless added coverage is purchased and notes homeowners may be responsible for repairs.
trust_notes: Useful national insurer education source.

title: Which disasters are covered by homeowners insurance? | III
claim_notes: Triple-I says sewer backups are not covered under a typical homeowners policy or flood insurance and usually require separate coverage or an endorsement.
trust_notes: Industry association summary source.

title: Interior Plumbing Lines Warranty | Plans & Coverage | AHS
claim_notes: AHS says its plans cover interior plumbing lines and sewer stoppages, but directs users to plan agreements for limitations and exclusions.
trust_notes: Useful for warranty-versus-endorsement caveat framing.

title: What Is a Sewer Scope Inspection, and Should You Get One? | Redfin
claim_notes: Redfin frames sewer scope inspection as a due-diligence tool buyers use to uncover hidden underground line issues before closing.
trust_notes: Useful buyer-intent source for transaction-safe inspection framing.

title: The Homes Americans Are Buying Are Older Than Ever | Redfin
claim_notes: Redfin reports that the median age of homes bought in 2024 hit a record 36 years nationally, with Buffalo, Pittsburgh, and Cleveland among the oldest metros in the dataset.
trust_notes: Useful demand proxy for old-house sewer-risk wedges in Rust Belt and Northeast metros.

title: Homeowners Emergency Loan Program (HELP) | City of Philadelphia
claim_notes: Philadelphia offers a zero-interest emergency repair loan program that can cover eligible water and sewer lateral repairs for owner-occupied homes.
trust_notes: Strong local conversion-support source for repair affordability and next-step framing.

title: The Philly region's homes for sale are getting old, and that's not a good thing | The Philadelphia Inquirer
claim_notes: The Inquirer reports Redfin data showing Philadelphia metro home sales skew heavily toward older housing, with a median sold-home age above 60 years in 2024.
trust_notes: Useful local market context source for buyer and old-house pages.

title: Overflow Action Day | MWRD
claim_notes: MWRD explains that most of the Chicago area uses combined sewers, so heavy rain can push stormwater and household wastewater through the same system and contribute to backups.
trust_notes: Strong local system-context source for Chicago buyer, backup, and cost pages.

title: Pipe Ownership | Pittsburgh Water
claim_notes: Pittsburgh Water states the entire sewer lateral from the building to the sewer main is the property owner's responsibility.
trust_notes: Strong local responsibility source for buyer and owner trust pages.

title: Water and Sewer Line Protection | Cleveland Water
claim_notes: Cleveland Water states many customers do not realize they are responsible for their water and sewer lines and promotes optional protection plans.
trust_notes: Useful local responsibility and cost-exposure source.

title: Sewer Backups | MSD Project Clear
claim_notes: MSD explains common sewer backup causes, stresses private-property maintenance, and points customers toward local response steps and program information.
trust_notes: Strong local system and responsibility source for St. Louis trust pages.

title: Stormwater Property Taxes and Sewer Lateral Programs | MSD Project Clear
claim_notes: MSD describes City and County sewer lateral repair fee programs that fund parts of the region's private lateral repair response.
trust_notes: Good local financing and program-structure source.

title: Sewer Backup | DC Water
claim_notes: DC Water says property owners are typically responsible for cleanup and most maintenance issues that cause backups, and that the utility generally does not pay for damages.
trust_notes: Strong local responsibility source with unusually clear utility language.

title: No Insurance? Sewer and water laterals | City of Milwaukee
claim_notes: Milwaukee says homeowners are responsible for the water service line and sewer lateral from their home to the public main, including repair and maintenance.
trust_notes: Official city utility wording that ties responsibility to owner-side laterals.

title: Private Property Infiltration & Inflow Reduction Program | City of Milwaukee
claim_notes: Milwaukee says its PPII Reduction Program addresses clearwater infiltration and inflow and notes that many homes built before 1954 had direct foundation-drain connections that may require rehabilitation.
trust_notes: Strong local system-context source for old-house and backup-risk pages.

title: Milwaukee Pipe Check | Housing Help MKE
claim_notes: Housing Help MKE describes Pipe Check support for sewer lateral inspection and repair costs for eligible Milwaukee homeowners.
trust_notes: Useful support source for affordability caveats and next-step framing.

title: Development Services | Metropolitan Sewer District of Greater Cincinnati
claim_notes: MSD says private building sewers are maintained by the owner at the owner's cost and expense, including segments under sidewalks or public right-of-way.
trust_notes: Strong official source for owner-side private building sewer responsibility.

title: Sewer Backup Program | Metropolitan Sewer District of Greater Cincinnati
claim_notes: MSD says sewer backups can come from public or private causes and that the utility investigates whether the problem is in the public sewer or a blocked private building sewer.
trust_notes: Useful local source for public-versus-private backup framing.

title: CSOs, SSOs and Public and Private Backups | Metropolitan Sewer District of Greater Cincinnati
claim_notes: MSD explains that combined sewers are still common in parts of the region and that heavy rain can contribute to both public and private backup conditions.
trust_notes: Adds local wet-weather and combined-sewer context for Cincinnati pages.

title: Water and Wastewater Regulations | Baltimore City Department of Public Works
claim_notes: Baltimore regulations define the sewer lateral from the sewer main to the property line as city-side infrastructure, while the building sewer from the property line to the building is private and owner-maintained.
trust_notes: Strong official source for split responsibility at the property line.

title: Sewage Backup Expedited Reimbursement Program | Baltimore City Department of Public Works
claim_notes: Baltimore offers expedited reimbursement for reasonable cleanup and disinfection expenses after verified capacity-related wet-weather sewage backups, subject to eligibility, reporting, and documentation rules.
trust_notes: Useful local source for wet-weather reimbursement caveats without implying blanket city liability.

title: Sewer Repair and Sewer Cleanout Installation Permit | City of Detroit
claim_notes: Detroit says a permit is required before sewer service repair, replacement, or sewer cleanout installation work begins.
trust_notes: Strong official source for Detroit permit and repair-process context.

title: Basement Backup Protection Program | City of Detroit
claim_notes: Detroit says its basement backup protection work includes CCTV inspection and repair or replacement of defective private sewer service lines in targeted neighborhoods and cases.
trust_notes: Useful local source for backup-risk and evidence-first next steps.

title: Private Sewer Repair Program | City of Detroit
claim_notes: Detroit says eligible homeowners in qualifying neighborhoods may receive free private sewer repairs through a city program, subject to block-by-block and area eligibility rules.
trust_notes: Strong local program source, but eligibility remains neighborhood- and program-specific.

title: Average Sewer Line Repair And Replacement Cost - Forbes Home
claim_notes: Forbes Home frames sewer replacement as highly variable, with strong sensitivity to line length, access, material, method, and restoration factors.
trust_notes: Use as a market sanity check, not as a universal truth.

title: How Much Does a Sewer Line Replacement Cost? (2026 Pricing) | This Old House
claim_notes: This Old House provides broad cost ranges, trenchless versus traditional per-foot comparisons, notes that sewer lines under slabs can reach roughly $300-$350 per linear foot, and explains that older homes with cast iron or clay may push replacement logic.
trust_notes: Use as a secondary market sanity check with explicit caveats.

title: How Much Does Trenchless Sewer Line Piping Cost? [2026 Data] | Angi
claim_notes: Angi's current trenchless guide says trenchless sewer pipe lining often lands around $1,900-$6,000 per project, with pipe lining commonly about $135-$150 per linear foot and pipe-bursting-plus-lining around $150-$190 per linear foot, while access and restoration still move the number.
trust_notes: Use as a method-cost sanity check, not as a precise local quote source.

title: Sewer Camera Inspection Cost [2026 Data] | Angi
claim_notes: Angi's current sewer camera inspection guide says a basic inspection often runs about $175-$800, while more extensive sewer inspections can reach roughly $270-$1,730 depending on accessibility and scope.
trust_notes: Use as an inspection-cost sanity check, not as a guaranteed local service price.
